ChatGPT said:

Thought for 16s

Yeah. Here’s the clean recap.

1) Environment / Python setup

I do not think we ever set up a formal venv, conda env, requirements.txt, or pyproject.toml together. From what I remember, we were just writing Python modules and running them directly.

So:

  • If you do not see a .venv/, environment.yml, requirements.txt, or pyproject.toml, then there probably is no reproducible environment set up yet.

  • Putting a venv inside the repo is not a good idea. It is machine-specific, large, and should not be committed.

  • The standard thing to do now is:

    • create a local .venv next to the repo,

    • add .venv/ to .gitignore,

    • add a requirements.txt or pyproject.toml.

Given what we built, the likely dependencies are roughly:

  • torch

  • rdflib

  • requests

  • maybe jupyter if you want notebook support

If jupyter command is not found, that usually means either:

  • it was installed in some old venv you are not currently in, or

  • it was never installed globally.

If you want to use notebooks again, after creating a new venv you’d do something like:

Bash
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install torch rdflib requests jupyter

Then:

Bash
jupyter notebook

or

Bash
python -m notebook

But unless you specifically want notebooks, you do not need Jupyter for this project. Most of the code we built was meant to run as normal Python modules.

2) Main entry point / where to run the code

The main entry point we ended up building was:

Bash
python -m src.compare_exact

That was the driver script for:

  • generating random graphs

  • generating random path patterns

  • compiling them to DAGs

  • running the GPU engine

  • optionally exporting RDF

  • optionally comparing against rdflib / Fuseki

  • optionally logging CSV

And later we added command-line arguments, so it should support things like:

Bash
python -m src.compare_exact --comparison none
python -m src.compare_exact --comparison fuseki
python -m src.compare_exact --comparison rdflib fuseki --csv-path results.csv

The important modules we had were roughly:

  • src/graph.py — KGraph, CSR graph representation

  • src/constraints.py — DAG node types

  • src/dag_eval.py — core scoring recurrence / GPU evaluation

  • src/dag_reasoner.py — wrapper API over concepts

  • src/random_graphs.py — random graph + pattern generation

  • src/patterns.py — compile PathPattern → constraint DAG

  • src/rdf_export.py — export KGraph → RDF

  • src/sparql_patterns.py — generate SPARQL from a pattern

  • src/fuseki_client.py — talk to Fuseki

  • src/compare_exact.py — main benchmark harness

If you want the one place to start reading code again, start with:

  1. src.compare_exact

  2. src.dag_reasoner

  3. src.dag_eval

That should bring most of the project back into focus quickly.

3) What the next tasks were when we left off

The main implementation direction we had reached was:

Already done

  • CSR graph storage

  • type matrix storage

  • constraint DAG representation

  • exact matching

  • partial typed path scoring

  • GPU-vectorized EXISTS_RESTRICTION

  • GPU-vectorized PATH_STEP

  • random graph / pattern generation

  • RDF export

  • SPARQL generation

  • rdflib comparison

  • Fuseki comparison

  • timing

  • CSV logging

  • hardware metadata in CSV

  • engine-only benchmark mode

Where we were headed next

The next likely tasks were:

A. Benchmarking / evaluation

This was the main next step.

  • run structured stress tests

  • compare:

    • engine only

    • rdflib

    • Fuseki

  • especially compare partial-score SPARQL vs GPU engine

  • generate more convincing plots / tables

B. Reproducibility cleanup

We had not yet really packaged the project. A good next task would be:

  • add requirements.txt

  • add .gitignore

  • maybe add a short README with:

    • how to create env

    • how to run benchmarks

    • how to start Fuseki

C. Performance refinement

We had improved the main kernels, but there was still room to:

  • check for remaining Python-side bottlenecks

  • make sure batching across concepts/layers is as parallel as possible

  • possibly benchmark larger graphs and memory limits more systematically

D. Partial-score comparison path

We had added a second benchmark path for partial SPARQL scoring, because that was a strong comparison point for your thesis story:

  • the SPARQL version is awkward and slow

  • the GPU engine handles it naturally

E. Writing / theory

On the theory side, we had also been working on:

  • recurrence relations

  • complexity section

  • feature support table

  • exact-mode consistency-preservation phrasing

  • positioning relative to OWL/DL

So depending on whether you want to get back into coding or writing first, the natural next step is either:

  • make the repo reproducible and rerun benchmarks, or

  • resume the paper/proposal writeup using the complexity / guarantees / terminology material we developed.

If you want, I can help you reconstruct a minimal requirements.txt and README next so the repo becomes runnable again quickly.


You said:

I want to try getting the project and paper to a point where I can get it published at ISWC. In terms of framing, how do you recommend that I approach describing what I\'ve accomplished and what the potential use is? I could be very narrow about the task it solves (typed semantic path matching) but describe the usefulness of that task and justify via examples and a specific evaluation (i.e. showing the usefulness of typed semantic path matching in its own right, via testing with a concrete domain such as cleaning up wikidata or LLM error checking or something). Or I could be much broader about potential usefulness, and try to position the project as a drop-in replacement for certain types of reasoning or validation problems: positioning it as a reasoner that performs reasoning on the GPU, with a fallback to pellet for anything not supported, or positioning it as a tool for SHACL validation, or some existing fuzzy logic, etc.Both of these separately require a lot of work. The first involves really well defining the use case and having a convincing evaluation. The second involves making sure the tools is very user friendly and has strong logical and theoretical justification for why it is able to do what it can do. I could also try doing something in between, or working with just what I have implemented right now, but that runs the risk of death by equivocation rather than staking out a strong claim under which the paper is the support for. What do you recommend based on what I\'ve done so far, what I\'ll need to do, and what the actual impact of this work could be to the broader semweb audience?
2/2

ChatGPT said:

You’re asking exactly the right strategic question. This is less about what the system can do and more about what claim you can convincingly defend at ISWC.

I’m going to be very direct, because this is where a lot of good work fails to land papers.

🧭 The core decision

You outlined two directions:

Option A — Narrow, well-defined task

“Typed semantic path matching (partial / approximate), evaluated convincingly.”

Option B — Broad system claim

“GPU reasoner / validation engine / SHACL replacement / fuzzy DL system.”

🔴 My recommendation (strongly):

Go with Option A (narrow + deep), and frame upward, not outward.

Then:

  • Prove one thing really well

  • Show it generalizes

  • Do not claim to replace OWL reasoners or SHACL

❗ Why Option B will likely fail (at ISWC)

If you position this as:

  • a reasoner,

  • a SHACL engine,

  • or a general-purpose DL replacement,

reviewers will ask:

  • Is it sound?

  • Is it complete?

  • What fragment of OWL does it implement?

  • How does it compare to Pellet / HermiT?

  • What are the semantics of negation, ∀, cardinality, etc.?

And right now, the honest answers are:

  • ❌ not sound in OWL sense

  • ❌ not complete

  • ❌ not a defined DL fragment

  • ❌ not compatible with OWA semantics

That invites rejection even if your system is excellent.

✅ Why Option A will work (if done well)

You already have something genuinely strong:

A GPU-accelerated, interpretable, approximate typed path matching framework
with clear advantages over SPARQL and DL reasoning for this task.

That is:

  • novel,

  • implementable,

  • measurable,

  • and not already solved cleanly in the literature.

🎯 The framing that fits ISWC best

Here’s the positioning I would recommend:

🔥 Core claim (what your paper is about)

Efficient, interpretable, approximate instance–concept matching via typed path constraints

Or more simply:

Fast approximate instance checking using typed path scoring on GPUs

💡 Key insight (this is your “idea”)

Many useful semantic web tasks reduce to:

  • checking whether an entity satisfies a structured pattern,

  • where exact logical entailment is either too strict or too expensive.

Your contribution:

We provide a scalable, interpretable, approximate alternative
that evaluates these patterns efficiently on large graphs.

🧱 Structure of the paper (what will make it work)

1. Problem definition (very important)

Define something like:

Approximate instance checking / semantic alignment

Formally:

  • Input: KG + concept (as structured constraints)

  • Output: score s(v,C)s(v, C)s(v,C)

Make this crisp. This is your anchor.

2. Method (you already have this)

  • DAG formulation

  • recurrence

  • GPU execution

  • CSR layout

  • scoring semantics

This section is already strong.

3. Guarantees (carefully phrased)

You already worked this out — this is great:

  • consistency-preserving (under monotone constraints)

  • interpretable (path witnesses)

  • not complete

This is a strength, not a weakness.

4. Evaluation (this is where acceptance is decided)

This is the part you must get right.

You need two kinds of evaluation:

(A) Systems evaluation (you mostly have this)

Show:

  • runtime vs Fuseki / rdflib

  • scaling with:

    • |V|

    • path length

    • ontology size

You already have excellent data here.

(B) Task-level evaluation (this is the missing piece)

You need one concrete use case where this is clearly useful.

Pick one, not three.

🎯 Best candidates for your work

1. Error detection / validation in noisy KGs

  • detect entities that “almost match” a concept but fail in some dimension

  • show:

    • top-k mismatches

    • explainability via paths

2. LLM output validation

  • LLM generates structured KG facts

  • your system checks:

    • does this entity behave like a “Vehicle” / “Person” / etc.?

This is very compelling right now

3. Wikidata cleanup / type suggestion

  • “this entity is probably a X with score 0.8”

  • show:

    • ranking quality

    • partial matches

4. Approximate SHACL-like validation

BUT:

  • do not claim to replace SHACL

  • instead say:

    “soft constraint validation”

🧠 The right level of ambition

You don’t need to prove everything.

You need to prove:

This is a new, useful primitive that:

  • scales,

  • is interpretable,

  • and solves a real problem better than existing tools.

⚖️ What to avoid (this matters a lot)

Avoid claims like:

  • “GPU reasoner”

  • “replacement for OWL reasoners”

  • “complete reasoning system”

  • “implements DL fragment X” (unless you formally prove it)

These will trigger defensive reviewers.

🧩 The “middle path” (what you hinted at)

You said:

maybe something in between?

Yes — but carefully:

✅ Do this:

  • Define a specific task

  • Show one strong application

  • Then say:

“This framework can also be applied to X, Y, Z”

❌ Don’t do this:

  • Try to prove all X, Y, Z in the same paper

🧭 Concrete recommendation for your paper

Title style:

GPU-Accelerated Typed Path Scoring for Approximate Instance Matching in Knowledge Graphs

Contributions:

  1. Formalization of typed path scoring as approximate instance checking

  2. DAG-based evaluation framework

  3. GPU implementation with linear scaling

  4. Empirical comparison vs SPARQL

  5. Demonstration on one real task

🧠 Final advice (this is the key insight)

Right now, your biggest risk is:

Trying to prove too much instead of proving one thing convincingly

If you nail:

  • one task,

  • one evaluation,

  • one clear claim,

this is absolutely publishable at ISWC.

If you want next steps

I can help you:

  • pick the best evaluation task and design the experiment,

  • write a 1-page problem framing section,

  • or draft a contribution statement that will pass reviewer scrutiny.

Just tell me 👍


You said:

i need help picking the evaluation task. Do you have any suggestions? Grounding llm output seems like a good choice as its highly topical, but idk what domain, specific use case, or datasets/ontologies to use it with

ChatGPT said:

This is exactly the right place to focus — your evaluation task will make or break the paper.

I’m going to give you a concrete recommendation, not just options, so you don’t get stuck exploring endlessly.

🎯 Recommendation (what you should actually do)

✅ Primary evaluation task:

LLM-generated knowledge graph validation using typed path constraints

This is:

  • topical (reviewers like it)

  • directly aligned with your method

  • easy to demonstrate value

  • doesn’t require perfect ground truth

💡 Why this fits your system perfectly

Your system is fundamentally:

“Given an entity, how well does it behave like a concept?”

LLMs are bad at:

  • enforcing structural constraints

  • maintaining consistent graph patterns

So your system becomes:

A structural sanity-check for LLM-generated knowledge

That is a very strong story.

🧱 Concrete evaluation setup (do this)

Step 1: Pick a domain (keep it simple)

🔥 Best choice: Wikidata-like schema (vehicles / people / organizations)

Why:

  • graph-shaped

  • well-known

  • easy to reason about

  • lots of existing structure

  • simple constraints

Step 2: Define a few concepts manually

You do NOT need full OWL ontologies.

Just define 3–5 concepts as constraint DAGs, e.g.:

Example: Vehicle

  • hasPart some Wheel

  • hasPart some Seat

Example: Person

  • hasOccupation some Occupation

  • bornIn some Location

Example: Organization

  • hasHeadquarters some Location

  • hasEmployee some Person

Keep them:

  • small

  • interpretable

  • clearly structured

Step 3: Generate LLM outputs

Prompt an LLM (GPT, etc.) to produce small KG fragments, e.g.:

“Generate RDF-style facts about a bicycle / company / person”

You’ll get outputs like:

  • some correct

  • some subtly wrong

  • some structurally inconsistent

This is exactly what you want.

Step 4: Convert to your KGraph format

  • parse triples → nodes, edges, types

  • assign class labels from the generated data

Step 5: Run your system

For each generated entity vvv:

Compute:

s(v,C)s(v, C)s(v,C)

for each concept.

Step 6: Evaluate usefulness

You now have three easy evaluation angles:

(A) Error detection (strongest)

Show:

  • Entities with low scores are often incorrect

Example:

EntityConceptScoreIssue
BicycleVehicle0.5Missing wheels
CompanyPerson0.2Wrong structure

This is intuitive and compelling.

(B) Ranking / classification

For each entity:

  • rank concepts by score

Show:

  • correct concept ranks highest most of the time

(C) Explainability (your secret weapon)

Show:

“Why did this fail?”

Because your system can show:

  • missing edges

  • wrong structure

  • partial matches

This is something embeddings / LLM scoring cannot do.

📊 What metrics to report

Keep it simple:

1. Accuracy / ranking

  • top-1 concept correct?

  • top-k accuracy

2. Score separation

  • correct vs incorrect score distributions

3. Qualitative examples

  • a few very clear cases

This matters a lot at ISWC.

🚫 What NOT to do

Avoid:

  • trying to use a full OWL ontology

  • trying to prove logical correctness here

  • trying to compare to Pellet

  • trying to do too many domains

🧠 Alternative (if you want something safer)

If LLM integration feels too messy:

Backup option:

Wikidata corruption / perturbation experiment

  • take real triples

  • randomly remove / corrupt some edges

  • show your system detects degradation

This is:

  • easier

  • cleaner

  • less flashy

🏁 My final recommendation

Do this:

Primary evaluation: LLM-generated KG validation (vehicles / people / organizations)
Secondary: synthetic corruption experiment (optional)

This gives you:

  • novelty (LLM angle)

  • rigor (controlled experiment)

  • clarity (simple concepts)

💬 If you want help next

I can help you:

  • design exact prompts for LLM generation

  • define 3–5 perfect constraint DAGs

  • write the evaluation section

  • or generate a figure/table that reviewers will love

Just tell me 👍


You said:

side note -- what exactly was preventing this approach from being used for SHACL constraint checking / validation in exact match mode?

ChatGPT said:

Great question—and it cuts right to the boundary of what you’ve built.

Short answer:

Nothing prevents you from using your system for SHACL-like validation in exact mode—but it does not implement full SHACL semantics, and that’s what prevents you from claiming equivalence or replacement.

Let’s make that precise.

🧭 What your system can do (SHACL-like)

In exact mode, your system already supports a large subset of what SHACL does:

✅ Supported naturally

  • Property existence constraints

    • SHACL: sh:minCount 1

    • You: ∃ p.C via max over neighbors

  • Typed path constraints

    • SHACL: path + sh:class

    • You: typed path DAG

  • Conjunction of constraints

    • SHACL shapes combine constraints → AND

    • You: min aggregation

  • Lower-bound cardinality

    • SHACL: sh:minCount n

    • You: count / normalization

  • Explainability

    • SHACL: reports violations

    • You: can show missing/weak paths

So yes:

You can implement a SHACL-style validator using your engine.

❗ What prevents it from being full SHACL validation

There are three fundamental gaps:

1. ❌ Closed World Assumption (CWA)

SHACL:

  • assumes closed world

  • if a required property is missing, that’s a violation

Your system:

  • operates under open-world-like assumptions

  • absence of evidence ≠ violation

Even in exact mode, your system says:

  • “no matching edge found” → score 0

  • but not necessarily “this is logically invalid”

Why this matters:

SHACL validation requires:

“If it’s not there, it fails.”

Your system says:

“If it’s not there, I can’t confirm it.”

2. ❌ Universal constraints (strict ∀)

SHACL:

turtle
sh:path ex:hasPart ;
sh:class ex:Wheel ;

means:

ALL values of hasPart must be Wheels

Your implementation:

LaTeX
\\min_{(v,p,u)} s_C(u)

This is only:

“All observed neighbors satisfy C”

But:

  • SHACL assumes completeness of data

  • your system does not enforce that

So:

  • extra unknown edges → SHACL cares

  • your system ignores

3. ❌ Negation / disjointness / strict violation semantics

SHACL supports:

  • sh:not

  • sh:disjoint

  • strict violation reporting

Your system:

  • can detect violations (score ↓)

  • but does not enforce binary failure semantics

This is a philosophical difference:

SHACLYour system
pass / failscore
hard constraintssoft constraints
validationalignment

4. ❌ Target selection / shape scoping

SHACL has:

  • sh:targetClass

  • sh:targetNode

  • complex targeting logic

Your system:

  • evaluates all nodes uniformly

This is easy to add, but currently not built-in.

🧠 The deeper reason (important insight)

SHACL is fundamentally:

constraint satisfaction under a closed-world model

Your system is:

constraint scoring under an open-world, monotone model

That difference is structural, not incidental.

🧩 So can you still use it for SHACL?

✅ Yes — with a reinterpretation:

Your system becomes:

Soft / approximate SHACL validation

Meaning:

  • instead of “valid / invalid”

  • you get:

    s(v,Shape)∈[0,1]s(v, \\text{Shape}) \\in [0,1]s(v,Shape)∈[0,1]

💡 How to frame this (if you want to mention it in the paper)

This is the correct and strong way to say it:

The proposed framework can be viewed as a soft generalization of SHACL validation, where constraint satisfaction is replaced by a graded alignment score. While the system does not assume closed-world semantics or enforce strict violation conditions, it enables scalable, interpretable validation-like reasoning over large graphs.

⚖️ Summary

You CAN:

  • emulate many SHACL constraints

  • use it as a validator

  • detect violations

  • provide explanations

  • scale far better than SHACL engines

You CANNOT claim:

  • full SHACL compliance

  • strict validation semantics

  • closed-world correctness

🧭 Practical takeaway

For your ISWC paper:

  • ❌ Don’t position it as “a SHACL engine”

  • ✅ Do say:

    “can be used for soft / approximate constraint validation”

If you want, I can help you:

  • map specific SHACL constraints → DAG nodes (table form)

  • or design a mini SHACL-style evaluation that fits your system perfectly without overclaiming

This is actually a really strong secondary framing if done carefully.


You said:

we had discussed this approach potentially being usable under CWA semantics, right?

ChatGPT said:

Yes — we did, and you’re remembering something important.
But the precise statement is:

✅ Your approach can be used under CWA semantics
❌ It does not inherently enforce CWA — you have to adopt it at the interpretation level

Let me make that distinction very clear, because it’s the key to reconciling everything we’ve said.

🧠 1. What changes under CWA?

Under Closed World Assumption (CWA):

“If a fact is not present in the graph, it is assumed to be false.”

That means:

  • Missing edge ⇒ violation

  • Missing type ⇒ violation

  • No neighbor ⇒ constraint failure

Under Open World (OWA):

Missing information is unknown, not false

⚙️ 2. What your system actually computes

Your system computes:

s(v,C)s(v, C)s(v,C)

based on:

  • observed edges

  • observed types

  • no inference about missing facts

So by default, it behaves like:

“evaluate constraints over observed data”

This is actually compatible with both OWA and CWA, depending on interpretation.

🔑 3. Why it works naturally under CWA

Here’s the key insight:

Your operators already behave like CWA if interpreted that way

Example: existential constraint

s(v)=max⁡(v,p,u)sC(u)s(v) = \\max_{(v,p,u)} s_C(u)s(v)=(v,p,u)max​sC​(u)

  • If no neighbors exist:

    • max over empty set = 0

Under CWA:

“no such edge exists” ⇒ violation
✔️ matches your behavior exactly

Example: conjunction

s(v)=min⁡isi(v)s(v) = \\min_i s_i(v)s(v)=imin​si​(v)

  • any missing requirement ⇒ 0

✔️ matches SHACL-style validation

Example: universal (your weak ∀)

s(v)=min⁡(v,p,u)sC(u)s(v) = \\min_{(v,p,u)} s_C(u)s(v)=(v,p,u)min​sC​(u)

Under CWA:

  • if any neighbor violates ⇒ fail

  • if no neighbors exist:

    • depends on convention (often vacuously true)

This is very close to SHACL semantics.

🧩 4. So what was the earlier caveat?

Earlier I said:

“You’re not doing SHACL because you don’t enforce CWA”

That was slightly imprecise.

More accurate:

You don’t explicitly model CWA — but your execution model is compatible with CWA if the input graph is treated as complete.

⚠️ 5. The real limitation (this is the important part)

Even under CWA, there are still differences from full SHACL:

(A) Explicit negation / disjointness

SHACL:

  • can say “must NOT have property X”

Your system:

  • can detect absence/presence

  • but doesn’t enforce global logical negation

(B) Shape targeting semantics

SHACL:

  • applies constraints to specific node sets

You:

  • evaluate all nodes uniformly

(easy to fix, but currently separate)

(C) Strict pass/fail vs score

SHACL:

  • binary validation

You:

  • continuous scoring

But this is actually a feature, not a limitation.

🎯 6. The correct, precise statement

This is what you should say in your paper:

When the input knowledge graph is interpreted under a closed-world assumption, the proposed framework can be used to perform constraint validation analogous to SHACL, with constraint satisfaction computed via exact scoring. In this setting, missing edges or types are treated as violations, and exact-mode scores correspond to constraint satisfaction.

🔥 7. Even stronger (and very publishable framing)

You can actually say something like:

The framework can be interpreted as a generalization of SHACL validation, extending binary constraint satisfaction to graded, interpretable scoring while retaining compatibility with closed-world validation semantics.

That’s a strong but defensible claim.

🧠 8. Key intuition (remember this)

  • Your system = constraint evaluator

  • OWA vs CWA = interpretation layer

You didn’t build an OWA system — you built a data-driven evaluator.

That’s why it works in both worlds.

🏁 Final answer

Yes — your approach can absolutely be used under CWA semantics, and in that setting it behaves very similarly to SHACL-style validation in exact mode. The limitation is not the execution model, but that you do not implement the full SHACL language or its complete logical semantics.

If you want next, I can help you:

  • write a short subsection: “Relationship to SHACL and CWA semantics”, or

  • design a clean SHACL-style example that demonstrates equivalence in exact mode

This could actually be a really nice supporting argument in your paper.


You said:

We proposed a method of extending this approach to cover explicit negation and disjointness, right?

ChatGPT said:

Yes — we did discuss a way to support negation/disjointness, but the key nuance (and this is important for your paper) is:

✅ You can evaluate negation and disjointness
❌ You cannot keep the same consistency guarantees when you include them

Let me reconstruct exactly what we proposed and how it fits into the current system.

🧠 1. What we proposed for negation

The idea was essentially:

Negation as score inversion

For a constraint CCC, define:

s¬C(v)=1−sC(v)s_{\eg C}(v) = 1 - s_C(v)s¬C​(v)=1−sC​(v)

So:

  • perfect match to CCC → 0

  • perfect non-match → 1

This is:

  • simple

  • GPU-friendly

  • compositional in the DAG

🧠 2. Disjointness via negation

If you have:

C1⊓C2⊑⊥C_1 \\sqcap C_2 \\sqsubseteq \\botC1​⊓C2​⊑⊥

you can encode:

svalid(v)=1−min⁡(sC1(v),sC2(v))s_{\\text{valid}}(v) = 1 - \\min(s_{C_1}(v), s_{C_2}(v))svalid​(v)=1−min(sC1​​(v),sC2​​(v))

Or equivalently:

  • penalize overlap between disjoint concepts

🧠 3. What this means semantically

This gives you:

A soft violation signal, not a logical prohibition

So instead of:

  • SHACL: “this node is invalid”

  • OWL: “this node causes inconsistency”

You get:

  • “this node violates constraints with score X”

⚠️ 4. Why this breaks the earlier proof

The consistency-preservation proof relied on:

Monotonicity

i.e., adding facts doesn’t invalidate previous satisfaction.

Negation is non-monotone:

  • adding a fact can invalidate a previously valid assignment

Example:

  • initially: sC(v)=0s_C(v) = 0sC​(v)=0 → s¬C(v)=1s_{\eg C}(v) = 1s¬C​(v)=1

  • later: we add evidence for CCC

  • now: s¬C(v)=0s_{\eg C}(v) = 0s¬C​(v)=0

So:

You can no longer guarantee that adding C(v)C(v)C(v) preserves consistency

🧩 5. So what did we actually “support”?

We supported:

✅ Computational support

  • negation nodes in the DAG

  • disjointness detection

  • violation scoring

❌ Logical guarantees

  • no longer consistency-preserving

  • no longer monotone

  • no longer tied to DL semantics

🎯 6. The correct way to describe this in your paper

This is the safe and strong wording:

The framework can be extended to support negation and disjointness via score inversion and penalty-based aggregation. However, these operators introduce non-monotonicity, and thus the consistency-preserving guarantees of the exact-mode semantics apply only to the positive (monotone) fragment.

🔥 7. Why this is still a strength (not a weakness)

This actually gives you something interesting:

ModeProperties
Positive fragmentconsistent, monotone, safe
With negationexpressive, diagnostic, violation-aware

So you can say:

The system provides a dual capability:

  • safe reasoning (positive constraints)

  • diagnostic reasoning (with negation)

That’s a nice story.

🧠 8. Important conceptual takeaway

Your system is not:

“a fuzzy DL reasoner”

It is:

a compositional constraint scoring system

That’s why negation works computationally but not logically.

🏁 Final answer

Yes — we proposed a way to support negation and disjointness using score inversion and penalty functions. However, these extensions break monotonicity and therefore invalidate the earlier consistency-preservation guarantees. They should be treated as diagnostic or scoring mechanisms rather than logically sound reasoning operators.

If you want next, I can:

  • write a clean table of supported operators vs guarantees for your paper, or

  • give you a short subsection on “Handling Negation and Disjointness” that fits perfectly after your method section


You said:

could we prove correctness under a CWA if we allow continuous computation -- i.e., you only stop once scores haven\'t changed between runs? i think i remember a conversation we had about this -- and it was guaranteed to terminate as well. This was because it used separate scores for CWA / OWA, and there were rules about which ones were allowed to increase or decrease. Obviously that\'s not what we actually implemented so far, but I\'m trying to remember what the fundamental constraints of the approach as a whole were, and whether there was anything really fundamental preventing it from being used for SHACL if the features we talked about were fully implemented.

ChatGPT said:

Yes — I think what you’re remembering is basically right, and it’s a useful way to think about the broader approach.

The short answer is:

Yes, in principle, you could recover a kind of correctness under a closed-world semantics by moving from one-shot DAG evaluation to an iterative fixpoint computation over a finite state space.

But that would be a different system from what you’ve implemented so far, and the proof would be for a restricted SHACL-like / rule-like fragment, not for arbitrary SHACL or OWL.

Here’s the clean version.

What we were talking about before

We had sketched a scheme where, instead of a single score s(v,C)s(v,C)s(v,C), each node–concept pair carries a state encoding something like:

  • whether vvv is currently believed to satisfy CCC,

  • whether that judgment is “open” or “closed,”

  • and possibly whether there is an explicit violation.

The simplest form was the two-bit idea you proposed, something like:

  • [0,0][0,0][0,0]: unknown / not yet supported

  • [1,0][1,0][1,0]: supported under closed-world assumptions

  • [0,1][0,1][0,1]: explicitly ruled out / contradiction / violation

  • [1,1][1,1][1,1]: strongly supported / exact / stable

The exact labels can vary, but the important part is that:

  • each (v,C)(v,C)(v,C) lives in a finite lattice of states,

  • update rules are monotone in that lattice,

  • and the system iterates until no state changes.

That is the reason termination was plausible.

Why termination was guaranteed

If you define:

  • a finite set of possible states for each (v,C)(v,C)(v,C),

  • and only allow updates that move “upward” in a partial order,

then the process must terminate, because:

  • there are only finitely many node–concept pairs,

  • each pair can only change state finitely many times,

  • and no rule is allowed to move backward.

This is the standard fixpoint argument.

Formally, if:

  • VVV is finite,

  • C\\mathbf{C}C is finite,

  • each (v,C)(v,C)(v,C) has state in a finite lattice LLL,

  • and your update operator

    F:L∣V∣×∣C∣→L∣V∣×∣C∣F : L^{|V|\\times |\\mathbf{C}|} \\to L^{|V|\\times |\\mathbf{C}|}F:L∣V∣×∣C∣→L∣V∣×∣C∣

    is monotone,

then repeated application

X(0)⊑X(1)⊑X(2)⊑⋯X^{(0)} \\sqsubseteq X^{(1)} \\sqsubseteq X^{(2)} \\sqsubseteq \\cdotsX(0)⊑X(1)⊑X(2)⊑⋯

must stabilize at a least fixpoint after finitely many steps.

So yes: termination was not the problem.

What this would buy you

This kind of iterative semantics is exactly what you would want if you were trying to support:

  • closed-world validation,

  • recursive propagation,

  • negative constraints,

  • disjointness,

  • “supported unless contradicted” style reasoning.

In other words, it is much closer to:

  • SHACL-style validation,

  • Datalog-like fixpoint reasoning,

  • stratified rule systems,

than to OWL tableaux.

So if you fully built that version, then yes, your overall approach could be used for a large class of SHACL-like tasks.

What the fundamental constraints were

The real constraints were not “GPU can’t do it” or “DAGs can never support SHACL.”

The real constraints were:

1. You need a monotone state update system

If negation/disjointness are handled by naive score inversion like

s¬C(v)=1−sC(v),s_{\eg C}(v)=1-s_C(v),s¬C​(v)=1−sC​(v),

that is not monotone, so fixpoint iteration can oscillate.

To make negation work in a provable way, you need to represent it as a state transition system, not as a plain numeric inverse.

2. You need a finite-state semantics

Continuous real-valued scores in [0,1][0,1][0,1] are fine for ranking, but they are much harder to use for a termination/correctness proof unless you quantize or otherwise constrain updates.

What made the earlier idea workable was that the “CWA/OWA” bits or tiered states formed a finite domain.

3. You need a restricted language fragment

You could plausibly support a substantial SHACL-like fragment, but not arbitrary features with easy proofs.

The safe fragment would look something like:

  • target selection,

  • conjunction,

  • disjunction,

  • path constraints,

  • class constraints,

  • lower/upper cardinality,

  • negation/disjointness,

  • recursion only if the update system remains monotone.

That is already a lot.

4. You need clear closed-world semantics

SHACL assumes the graph is complete for validation purposes.

Your current engine is compatible with that interpretation, but it does not itself enforce it. The fixpoint version would need to explicitly say:

absence of evidence counts as failure for required facts.

That is a semantic choice, not a computational limitation.

So could this become a SHACL engine in principle?

I would say:

Yes, in principle, a fully implemented fixpoint version of your approach could support a substantial SHACL-like validation fragment on the GPU.

What would prevent you from claiming “full SHACL” is not a fundamental issue with the approach, but the amount of formalization and implementation work required:

  • define the exact fragment,

  • define the state lattice,

  • prove monotonicity of all update rules,

  • prove termination,

  • prove correspondence to SHACL semantics for that fragment.

That is a major project, but not obviously impossible.

Why this is different from what you have now

Your current implementation is:

  • one-pass or layerwise DAG scoring,

  • mostly acyclic,

  • mostly positive constraints,

  • score-valued,

  • designed for ranking and matching.

The hypothetical SHACL/fixpoint version would be:

  • iterative until convergence,

  • finite-state or carefully stratified,

  • closed-world,

  • validation-oriented,

  • with proofs of termination and correctness.

So the approach as a whole is broader than the current prototype.

The clean conclusion

I think the most accurate way to say it is:

The current implementation is not a SHACL validator, but the underlying approach does not fundamentally preclude SHACL-like validation. In particular, if extended with a monotone fixpoint semantics over a finite state lattice encoding support and violation under a closed-world interpretation, the method could support a substantial validation fragment with guaranteed termination.

That is strong, honest, and technically plausible.

If you want, I can help reconstruct that earlier fixpoint idea more formally as:

  • a state lattice,

  • update rules,

  • and a termination theorem sketch.
